channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Cosmic Distance Ladder
item.description:
The cosmic distance ladder is the sequence of techniques used to measure distances in the universe, each method calibrating the next: parallax to Cepheid variables to Type Ia supernovae to the Hubble flow.

No single technique works at all scales. Parallax is geometrically precise but only reaches nearby stars (up to a few kiloparsecs with current instruments). Cepheid variable stars – whose pulsation period correlates with luminosity – extend the range to nearby galaxies (~30 megaparsecs). Type Ia supernovae, brighter and visible at cosmological distances, extend the range to billions of light-years. At the greatest distances, redshift and the Hubble parameter provide distance estimates.

item.title: Gravitational Lensing
item.description:
Gravitational lensing is the bending of light by massive objects, predicted by general relativity. When light from a distant source passes near a massive foreground object (a galaxy, galaxy cluster, or other concentration of mass), the curvature of spacetime deflects the light’s path, distorting, magnifying, or multiplying the image of the background source.

Three regimes are distinguished:

Strong lensing. The foreground mass is dense enough to produce multiple images, arcs, or complete rings (Einstein rings) of the background source. Galaxy clusters frequently produce strong lensing.

item.title: Parallax
item.description:
Parallax is the apparent shift in position of a nearby object against a distant background, caused by a change in the observer’s viewpoint. In astronomy, stellar parallax uses Earth’s orbit around the Sun as a baseline to measure distances to nearby stars.

As Earth orbits the Sun, a nearby star appears to shift slightly against the background of much more distant stars. The shift is tiny – measured in arcseconds (1/3600 of a degree). The parallax angle pp (half the total apparent shift over six months) is related to distance by d=1/pd = 1/p, where dd is in parsecs and pp is in arcseconds. One parsec (about 3.26 light-years) is the distance at which a star has a parallax of one arcsecond.

item.title: Standard Candle
item.description:
A standard candle is an astronomical object whose intrinsic luminosity is known (or can be inferred from other observable properties), allowing its distance to be calculated from how bright it appears.

The principle is straightforward: if you know how much light a source actually emits (its luminosity) and you measure how much light you receive (its apparent brightness), the inverse-square law gives the distance. Apparent brightness falls off as the square of the distance, so d=L/4πFd = \sqrt{L / 4\pi F}, where LL is luminosity and FF is observed flux.

item.title: Type Ia Supernova
item.description:
A Type Ia supernova is a thermonuclear explosion of a white dwarf star that reaches a nearly uniform peak luminosity, making it a reliable standard candle for measuring cosmological distances.

The standard model: a white dwarf in a binary system accretes matter from a companion star. When the white dwarf’s mass approaches the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses), carbon and oxygen in its interior ignite in a runaway thermonuclear detonation. The explosion completely destroys the white dwarf.
